did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a hybrid AI system combining YOLOv8m for real-time lesion detection and EfficientNet-B0 for seven-class dermatoscopic image classification on the HAM10000 dataset. It also employs a meta-classifier for risk stratification and Grad-CAM for interpretability, achieving 94.4% accuracy.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development and validation of a hybrid AI system (YOLOv8m and EfficientNet-B0) for dermatological condition recognition. It details a specific methodology including dataset usage (HAM10000), model architecture, training strategies, and reports new empirical results such as 94.4% accuracy, rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: A Hybrid AI System for Real-Time Dermatological Condition Recognition and Interpretability Using YOLOv8 and EfficientNet-B0
journal: International Journal on Science and Technology (IJSAT)
brief_summary: This study presents a hybrid AI system for real-time dermatological condition recognition that integrates YOLOv8m for lesion detection and EfficientNet-B0 for seven-class classification on the HAM10000 dataset. The framework enhances clinical utility by incorporating a meta-classifier that fuses image features with patient metadata (age, sex, lesion site) to stratify risk into low, medium, or high categories. Interpretability is addressed through Grad-CAM visualizations, which are embedded in a Flask-based web interface to support longitudinal patient tracking. The system was evaluated using standard metrics including accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score across the seven lesion classes. It achieved an overall accuracy of 94.4%, demonstrating robust performance on both common and rare lesion types. The results indicate that this interpretable, ready-for-use system offers a practical solution for AI-assisted diagnosis in clinic and teledermatology settings.

all_task_types.details: YOLOv8m is used for real-time lesion detection, outputting bounding boxes for candidate lesions with confidence scores.

EfficientNet-B0 classifies cropped lesion regions into one of seven discrete categories (akiec, bcc, bkl, df, mel, nv, vasc) from the HAM10000 dataset. The system also uses a meta-classifier to output final class labels.
A meta-classifier fuses image features and patient metadata (age, sex, lesion site) to stratify risk into low, medium, or high categories based on clinical relevance.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: A public dataset comprising 10,015 dermatoscopic images of pigmented lesions categorized into seven classes, including tabular metadata such as patient age, sex, and anatomical site.

use.training: Used for training YOLOv8m and EfficientNet-B0 models with an 80-20 stratified split for training and validation sets.
use.validation: Used for validation to monitor loss and macro F1-score during training and to report overall accuracy of 94.4%.
use.testing: Used for final evaluation of class-wise metrics (precision, recall, F1-score) and confusion matrix analysis.
use.other: Used for comparative benchmarking against other architectures like ResNet, DenseNet, and Inception.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To divide the HAM10000 dataset into training and validation sets (80-20 split) while maintaining class proportions.
To obtain stable estimates by averaging results from multiple runs with different random seeds.
To tune hyperparameters such as learning rates and weight decay for optimizers (Adam and SGD).
To train the meta-classifier on fused features and avoid information leakage.
To address class imbalance during the training of the EfficientNet-B0 classifier.
To prevent overfitting based on validation loss and macro F1-score.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, and confusion matrices, with stability assessed by averaging results from multiple runs with different random seeds.

HAM10000.notes: The system ranks among the top dermatology AI methods on the HAM10000 dataset. Results were obtained by averaging multiple runs with different random seeds to ensure stability.

HAM10000_Class_Wise_Performance.notes: nv and vasc classes exhibit very high precision and recall (F1-scores around 0.93–0.95). bkl and bcc achieve strong F1-scores in the mid-to-high 0.7 range. df achieves perfect precision (1.00) but lower recall (0.64). mel shows slightly lower recall (0.62) due to class imbalance and visual overlap with nv. The main off-diagonal confusion occurs between mel and nv.

Comparative_Benchmarking.notes: The proposed system's 94.4% accuracy and robust class-wise F1-scores place it at the upper end of reported performances compared to conventional single-model CNNs and architectures like ResNet, DenseNet, and Inception.

architecture_used.main_model: Hybrid system combining YOLOv8m for detection and EfficientNet-B0 for classification, followed by a meta-classifier for risk stratification.
architecture_used.pretraining: YOLOv8m and EfficientNet-B0 were fine-tuned from pretrained weights (normalized using ImageNet mean and standard deviation).
architecture_used.input_size: YOLOv8m input: 640x640 pixels; EfficientNet-B0 input (cropped lesions): 224x224 pixels.

architecture_details.fusion_component: Meta-classifier (logistic regression or shallow fully connected neural network) fusing classification probabilities, detection confidence, and patient metadata (age, sex, lesion site).
architecture_details.interpretability_module: Grad-CAM applied to the final convolutional layers of EfficientNet-B0.

training_details.dataset_split: 80-20 stratified split for training and validation.
training_details.optimizers: Adam and SGD with momentum (learning rates and weight decay tuned via grid search).

loss_functions.YOLOv8m: Standard object detection losses (localization, confidence, classification).

training_details.regularization: Early stopping (based on validation loss and macro F1-score), Dropout, and data augmentation (random rotations, flips, brightness/contrast adjustments, cropping).
training_details.meta_classifier_training: Trained on fused features using cross-validation to avoid information leakage.

classification_or_output_head.classes: Seven classes: actinic keratosis (akiec), basal cell carcinoma (bcc), benign keratosis-like lesions (bkl), dermatofibroma (df), melanoma (mel), melanocytic nevi (nv), vascular lesions (vasc).
classification_or_output_head.risk_output: Discrete risk categories: low, medium, high.

preprocessing_techniques: Image Resizing: Images resized to 640x640 pixels for YOLOv8m input; lesion crops resized to 224x224 pixels for EfficientNet-B0 input.
Normalization: Images normalized using ImageNet mean and standard deviation.
Data Augmentation: Random rotations, horizontal and vertical flips, brightness and contrast adjustments, and slight cropping applied to mitigate overfitting.
Metadata Encoding: Age normalized to a range; Sex encoded as binary or one-hot feature; Lesion site one-hot encoded.

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied to increase robustness and mitigate overfitting.

conclusions: The proposed hybrid AI system, integrating YOLOv8m for real-time lesion detection and EfficientNet-B0 for seven-class classification, achieved an overall accuracy of 94.4% on the HAM10000 dataset.
The system maintains good precision and recall for both common and rare lesion types, ranking among the top dermatology AI methods on the HAM10000 dataset.

Incorporating patient metadata (age, sex, lesion location) via a meta-classifier allows for risk stratification into low, medium, or high categories, aligning model outputs more closely with clinical decision-making.
The integration of Grad-CAM visualizations directly into a Flask-based web interface enhances interpretability and transparency, allowing clinicians to verify the system's reasoning.

The modular design supports real-time inference and longitudinal patient tracking, offering a practical and dependable solution for AI-assisted dermatological diagnosis in clinic and teledermatology settings.

The study acknowledges that while it addresses challenges like metadata integration and interpretability, it does not fully resolve systemic issues such as dataset bias toward lighter skin types or the need for external validation.
important_remarks: The study relies exclusively on the HAM10000 dataset, which is known to have a bias toward lighter skin types, limiting the generalizability of the results to diverse populations.
The paper explicitly acknowledges that it does not fully resolve systemic issues such as dataset bias toward lighter skin types or the need for external validation on independent clinical datasets.

Performance on rare lesion classes is limited by small sample sizes in the HAM10000 dataset (e.g., dermatofibroma support of 11 and vascular lesions support of 14), which may affect the reliability of metrics for these classes.

The meta-classifier was trained using cross-validation to avoid information leakage, but the specific details of the fusion weights and calibration with clinicians are noted as potential areas for future refinement.

The system is presented as a proof-of-concept web application (Flask-based) rather than a clinically validated regulatory-approved device, highlighting a gap between research prototype and clinical deployment.

Class-wise performance shows significant variance, with melanoma recall at 0.62 and F1-score at 0.68, indicating potential risks for false negatives in high-stakes diagnoses despite the high overall accuracy of 94.4%.
